body_html:
Page 1

Bearing 315
The Newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club
November - December, 1990

COMING EVENTS

HAMLIN PARK
Saturday, November 17
Debbie Newell is Meet Director and Don Denbi is course designer for Hamlin Park on November 17. Hamlin Park, at N 155th and 15th NE, is one of our older, black and white maps, but there are lots of contours, and lots of runnable area, and lots of sports fields. With Debbie and Dopn, this should be a good meet.

FARREL-McWHIRTER
Saturday, December 8
Farrel-McWhirter Park is located several miles east of Redmond off Avondale Raod. The map was updated and reprinted this year. With lots of nettles and low brush, this is a good map to run on in winter. This is also the 2nd of the WIOL (Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League) meets.

BOG-SLOG
Sunday, December 30
This is the 11th Annual Bog Slog, Pool Swim and Holiday Bash Potluck at St Edwards State Park. The Park is about a mile south of Kenmore on Juanita Drive. Don Atkinson will be course designer, so we can look for some "Australian design" courses. Following your run, try the pool. The pool will be open to the general public ($2) from 11:30 to 1:30. Bring your own suit and towel. From 2 until ?, we will gather at Debbie Newell's at 6216 NE 154th (off of Arrowhead Drive, the next road on Juanita Drive north from the St Edwards road). This is a potluck, and has seen fantastic food in past years. Pies will be provided as prizes for class winners. This is not only a good meet site (we will have an updated map), but a great fun event too.

NO EVERGREEN CUP
There will be no Evergreen Cup over the Thanksgiving weekend this year, and no WIOL Clinic. The first WIOL meet will be the Kelsey Creek meet, Sat, Dec 1

LOST EXTENSION CORDS
Several electrical extension cords were misplaced at APOC at Cle Elum. If you know of the location of any of these cords, please call Jean Davis at 781-9562

CLUB NOTES

BOARD BRIEFS
October 2
The Board met October 2 at Doug and Katie Sprugel's. Present were Mike Schuh, Harvey Friedman, Bruce McAlister, Don Atkinson, David Soper, Denise Klein, Katie Sprugel and Ardis Dull. Bruce reported on Drunken Charlie (good weather and 34 orienteers), and Denise reported on preparations for Ft. Casey and UW Campus. Various possibilities for 1991 scheduling were discussed, including a mini-rogaine at Tumac Mountain. Get your schedule preferences to the Board.

November 7
The Board met November 7 at Bruce McAlister's. Present were Mike Schuh, Bruce McAlister, Debbie Newell, Don Atkinson and Harvey Friedman. There were reports on the Ft Casey and UW meets, and Debbie reported on the coming Hamlin Park meet. Bruce reported on preparations for the Bog Slog. The next Board meeting will be held December 12 at 7:30 at Mike Schuh's. All club members are welcome to attend all Board Meetings

MONTHLY CLUB MEETING
Psychology Report
Election of Officers
By-law Change
The regular bi-monthly club meeting will be held on Thursday, November 29 at 7:00 pm at the University Library at NE 50th and Roosevelt Way. Dr. Susan Infield of the UW Psychology Department will be present and will review the conclusions from her study of orienteers. And, if it's Club Meeting in November, it must be elections. As was indicated last issue, Mike Schuh has indicated he would prefer to take a less active role next year. Several people hve expressed a willingness to serve as board members, but as of publication, we do not have a complete slate. As to Board members, Denise Klein and David Soper have agreed to continue as Event Coordinators, Debbie Newell as Mapping Coordinator, Don Atkinson as equipment and Harvey Friedman as Membership. New Board assignments include Dan Klein who will work with the Newsletter in January (and become Editor in March), Bruce McAlister will serve as one of the WSOA representatives, and Marjorie Bone will be the new Hotline coordinator.
There is still lots of opportunity to help on the Board, and if you are interested in helping as an assistant, or taking charge in an area,let us know, and you will have a job.

Page 2

Monthly Club Meeting - Continued
Proposed By-Law Change
A by-laws amendment will be proposed at the annual meeting to elect a Board instead of officers. Officers would then be elected annually by the newly elected Board. This would allow for more flexibility, and avoid the annual hunt for nominating officers.
Everyone is urged to come to the meeting to review the past and decide the future of Cascade Orienteering.

[Photo: Anne York, Harvey Friedman and Carl McNabb at Philmont. The "Tooth of Time" in the background]

PHILMONT

October 6/7
by Bruce McAlister

The temperature read 89 on the readerboard as we arrived at Denver Airport Friday afternoon. A short trip to Colorado Springs brought us to "Pinetum", the Colorado Springs home of Pat Albright and Peter Zurcher for supper and conversation.
Then up early and down to New Mexico. Philmont is a Scout ranch in the northwest corner of New Mexico near Taos with terrain very reminiscent of Cle Elum Ridge, but at about 7000 ft, and with rugged scenery. The course included pine, scrub oak and aspen forest, some open fields, and lots of steep terrain with sharp gullies. At Philmont we met Anne York and Harvey Friedman who came up from Albuquerque where they had been visiting Anne's relatives.
The weather was warm and sunny Saturday with cobalt blue skies, white mountains and golden aspen. Saturday was run on the Crater Lake map. When I first saw the map, I thought fantastic, no green !! Then I realized that they just hadn't printed vegetation on the map. Actual vegetation was much like Ross-Elum, with very green areas, and deep, narrow gullies.
The weather Sunday was lightly overcast and cooler. The terrain was similar to Day 1, but there was a good, new, color map, Urraca Mesa. The courses were challenging both days. Ten of 23 were DNF on the Green and Orange courses on Day 1; Hanna North was 2:38 on Blue; Svere Froyen was 2:09 on Red, and most of the others who finished were over 3 hours. Harvey was 1st of ten on Orange for the combined 2 days. Although difficult, they were well set and fun courses. There were few trails; legs were varied; short and long, and required several route choices, with disaster awaiting the wrong choice. It might have been better to have made the Orange course a little easier. It would be interesting to set at least one course like this at Ross-Elum this spring.
Tired, but happy, we left Philmont late Sunday for the three hour drive back to Denver. The clouds grew darker as we drove on, and the first swirling snow flakes of winter were falling as we drove into the airport.

US CHAMPIONSHIPS

The US Championships this year, hosted by HVO, were held at Harriman State Park, near West Point, New York on November 3, 4. Eleven local orienteers travelled east (E. Breseman, B. McAlister, D. Enger, D. Newell, R. Tharp, D. Tallent, B. MacDonald Sr, C. Moore and his wife Linda, B. Macdonald Jr, and F Veler), and were rewarded by two days of great orienteering. The weather was warm and clear, and the leaves were red and gold, and still mainly on the trees. The area, just west of West Point was largely open, rocky terrain, it offerred challenges, but was not as daunting as much of our local terrain. You can check out the map and courses at the Bog Slog Potluck. Our only local national champion this year is Fred Veler who placed first in M45A. The US Championships next year will be held in Missouri in early October, hosted by SLOC.

ORIENTEERING SKILLS CLINIC

Peter Zurcher of the Rocky Mountain Orienteering Club and former Swiss National O'Team member will conduct an orienteering skills clinic early in 1991. Although details are not yet final, it will probably be in February at Drunken Charlie Lake and Cle Elum. Look for more details in the next issue of "Bearing 315". Space in the class may be limited, so, if you are interested, please call Debbie Newell at 488-7471.

Questionnaire Coming

Rick Hood has prepared aquestonairre to identify the number and type of activities, COC members would like, and the resources available within the Club. The results will be reported in Bearing 315.

Page 3

Meet Results

CLE ELUM RIDGE - 30 SEPT 1990

Late September brings a gasp of pleasant weather to the eastern Cascades. By the time you read this freezing nights and the probability that precipitation will be snow are the actuality. EOC's last meet of 1990 was the last good day. Previous, and unusual, rain fooled some wildflowers into thinking it was Spring: patches of green, yellow, and blue were noticeable if you could get your mind off the search for orange and white.

A beginners; an intermediate (which was too easy), and a score course were available. In addition there was the opportunity to design your own course from the available score controls. Ann York, in a class by herself as usual, took advantage of this, putting her WC-PNWOF course setting skills to her own use.

She actually picked the least climb given thir placement. Her course conformed to the guidelines of the SOFT's book Course Planning.

BOY SCOUT COURSE
At the Saturday, January 5, 1991 Cascade Club meet at Woodland Park, one of the courses will be designed to satisfy the new Boy Scout requirement for a one-mile Scout level orienteering course. For details, call course designer James Siscel at 778-7202. We encourage Scout Troops who are members of Cascade or one of the other local orienteering clubs, to turn out for this meet. Other Scouts are, of course, also welcome.

Page 4

Letters to the Editor

My Dear Editor:

Please use correct terminology where possible in Bearing 315. Those cyyptic symbols relating to couse control points on advanced courses or those words describing where the control marker is within the control circle are control descriptions, not clues. Please never refer to control description sheets as clue sheets; you do not want to give new people the wrong idea about our sport. Just because ONA provides misleading terminology does not mean Cascade OC has to. I hope the terminology is used correctly in future editions of Bearing 315. Thank you.
HF

Cascade Chatter

compiled by Harvey Friedman

As usual, we'll start with positive roster changes as of 6 November 1990. Words of both thanks and welcome go to the following new or renewing club members: Boy Scout Troop 270, Susan Koscak, Michelle McMorran, Ross Stevens, Lloyd Sullivan. If you all don't come to meets, you'll probably not see your name in the newsletter until the next time you renew; also, membership cards are only available in person at a club function such as regular meet, meeting, pot-O-luck, but not at any ski-O meets, nor night-O meets, nor canoe-O meets (unless you are lucky), nor training events, nor workshops. If you move, don't forget to turn in a change of address card; you don't want your news-letter to be late (or lost).
Now on to the fun stuff...Orienteering is a sport of navigation, true, but it is more than just going thru the woods with a map; making new friends and renewing acquaintances with familiar faces are also important factors making O' the wonderful activity that it is. Families meeting families make for travel experiences to other countries that might be otherwise too costly to afford. Contact me if you need more information....If you want more news about people in the club, you'll have to tell me something or preferably write or scribble me something that is for this column since I have such a bad memory. Only news item submitted is that Angela Tallent has qualified for the cross-country nationals, and will be off to Omaha. Good luck Angela.

CALVIN AND HOBBES / BILL WATTERSON
Comic Strip
Panel 1: "I PERFORMED A SCIENTIFIC EXPERIMENT TODAY."
Panel 2: "YOU KNOW HOW MAPS ALWAYS SHOW NORTH AS UP AND SOUTH AS DOWN? I WANTED TO SEE IF THAT WAS TRUE OR NOT."
Panel 3: "WHAT DID YOU FIND OUT?"
Panel 4: "NOT MUCH. YOUR COMPASS DIDN'T SURVIVE THE TRIP SOUTH FROM THE TOP OF THE TREE."
Panel 5: "LET ME KNOW WHEN YOU GET A NEW ONE. MY JUNIOR SCIENTIST BOOK SAYS NOT TO GET DISCOURAGED BY TEMPORARY SETBACKS."

PNWOF - "The race is over"
- A Norwegian's View of PNWOF

The article on Page 7 "The Race is Over " is a translation of an article from the Norwegian orienteering magazine Orientering. Much of the article is about the World Cup race at Cle Elum. The editors guessed wrong, however, about the race being over. Ragnhild B. Anderson of Norway continued in first and won the overall women's competition. However, the "hypothetical possibility occurred in the men's race with Havard Tveite coming on with a fine finish in the last three races to win the men's competition.

Travel Coordination

Interested in travelling to A meets in New England or California or Europe? Even with the United discount, fares are high. To help out, Bruce McAlister is taking names of both those interested in out-of-town meets, and those who have unused tickets or coupons. He will also watch out for special excursion fares. O-meets are excellent candidates for excursion fares. However, these fares are usually available for only a few days at a time.

Possibilities are Texas Champs, New England, and the O'Ringen Swedish 5 day and the Scottish 6-day. Let Bruce know if you're interested in attending, or if you have any coupons you can't use.

Page 6

Translated and reprinted from the Norwegian Orienteering Magazine Orientering

The race is over

BERNT O. MYRVOLD

In the World Cup, the best five out of a total of eight races are counted. The competition is taking place in three regions with three runs in Northern Europe in the spring (Poland, Denmark, Norway); two runs in North America in the summer (Alberta/Canada and Washington/USA); and three runs in Central Europe in the fall (Switzerland, France, Germany). For all practical purposes, the winners have already been decided after the first five races.

In the mens class, Niklas Löwegren now has three first place, one fourth place and one twelfth place finish, and already has 187 points. Although a total of seven runners theoretically could pass Niklas if they would win all of the three remaining races, this is now only a hypothetical possibility. In the womens class, Ragnhild Bente Andersen has garnered 164 points and is 14 points ahead of the next runner. There are ten or twelve runners who have a theoretical possibility to end up with more points, but the problems any of these must overcome to win are of the same order of difficulty as for Norway's soccer team to make the world championship final after the results of their first two qualifying meets.

The race which took away much of the excitement from the World Cup took place at Cle Elum in the State of Washington on the USA's west coast. Orienteering is not a strong sport in the USA, and most of the o-runners to be found there live on the east coast, or, as one of the local runners said "half way to Norway". Five local clubs had gone together in order to manage all the arrangements for the World Cup. Still, there were many "Tordenskiolds" soldiers on the arrangements staff. The same people manned the loudspeakers, directed parking, manned the finish and hung out the controls. Meanwhile lightly stressed runners tried to catch the announcements, find a parking place, and find the assembly area or find the start. With eight hundred registered participants in addition to direct class, the meet was the largest which has been arranged in the USA - and maybe a little large for the arrangers.

The terrain which had been chosen covered a steep slope, part with narrow ravine-gullys and dense vegetation including areas with singularly fine detail, and part on a flat level area with open pine forest. Both parts were scored throughout by hundreds of small paths which were used by motorcyclists. The area had been described as comparable to "Norwegian terrain", and that proved to be an apt description. The course legs combined terrain types, and the courses were varied, combining stretches of demanding route choice with straight forward stretches, and used legs of varying length. The course designers had also boldly tried to use some of the "solid green" areas to provide a change of tempo. Unfortunately, one leg of one course was marred by having the control hidden away in dense brush where there was a minimal possibility of seeing through to the control. Here it would have been better to have had a more conventional course leg, or a controller who would have dared to step in and take over. Although a number of runners described some of the controls as bingo controls, the result list provided few surprises. The one surprise that stood out clearly was Tomas Prokes. With a 25th place finish, he dropped from third to seventh in the World Cup ratings. But the twenty year old had already shown at Voss that he was still not experienced enough to master Norwegian terrain. After the race, Niklas Löwegren and the Swedish team captain were smiling about the competition. An almost error free race resulted in a new win, and a whole four and one half minutes in front of the closest Norwegian competitor, and, not the least, 40 valuable new World Cup points. The Norwegian men had all made a number of errors, and were generally upset with themselves. However, with a second and third place to Havard Tveite and Rolf Vestre, both continue to have a theoretical possibility to bring home a victory in the final count.

It was a long day of waiting for Kerstin Haglund. With a start time early in the second draw, she crossed both the halfway point and the finish with what clearly a winning race time. However, most of the acknowledged strong competitors started in the third (and last) draw. One after the other, however, they arrived at the mid-point too late to challenge her time. The closest was Great Britain's Yvette Hague, 45 seconds behind at the midpoint. On the back half of the course, this year's student world champion lost almost the same amount, and was one and a half minutes behind at the finish. Late starting Ragnhild Bente Andersen came on very strongly at the end, and was only six seconds from victory at the finish. She finished in second place, and doubled her lead over Katerina Bork in the World Cup standings.

block_index.text: Bearing 315 The Newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club November - December, 1990

HAMLIN PARK Saturday, November 17 Debbie Newell is Meet Director and Don Denbi is course designer for Hamlin Park on November 17. Hamlin Park, at N 155th and 15th NE, is one of our older, black and white maps, but there are lots of contours, and lots of runnable area, and lots of sports fields. With Debbie and Dopn, this should be a good meet.

FARREL-McWHIRTER Saturday, December 8 Farrel-McWhirter Park is located several miles east of Redmond off Avondale Raod. The map was updated and reprinted this year. With lots of nettles and low brush, this is a good map to run on in winter. This is also the 2nd of the WIOL (Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League) meets.

BOG-SLOG Sunday, December 30 This is the 11th Annual Bog Slog, Pool Swim and Holiday Bash Potluck at St Edwards State Park. The Park is about a mile south of Kenmore on Juanita Drive. Don Atkinson will be course designer, so we can look for some "Australian design" courses. Following your run, try the pool. The pool will be open to the general public ($2) from 11:30 to 1:30. Bring your own suit and towel. From 2 until ?, we will gather at Debbie Newell's at 6216 NE 154th (off of Arrowhead Drive, the next road on Juanita Drive north from the St Edwards road). This is a potluck, and has seen fantastic food in past years. Pies will be provided as prizes for class winners. This is not only a good meet site (we will have an updated map), but a great fun event too.
NO EVERGREEN CUP There will be no Evergreen Cup over the Thanksgiving weekend this year, and no WIOL Clinic. The first WIOL meet will be the Kelsey Creek meet, Sat, Dec 1
LOST EXTENSION CORDS Several electrical extension cords were misplaced at APOC at Cle Elum. If you know of the location of any of these cords, please call Jean Davis at 781-9562

BOARD BRIEFS October 2 The Board met October 2 at Doug and Katie Sprugel's. Present were Mike Schuh, Harvey Friedman, Bruce McAlister, Don Atkinson, David Soper, Denise Klein, Katie Sprugel and Ardis Dull. Bruce reported on Drunken Charlie (good weather and 34 orienteers), and Denise reported on preparations for Ft. Casey and UW Campus. Various possibilities for 1991 scheduling were discussed, including a mini-rogaine at Tumac Mountain. Get your schedule preferences to the Board.

November 7 The Board met November 7 at Bruce McAlister's. Present were Mike Schuh, Bruce McAlister, Debbie Newell, Don Atkinson and Harvey Friedman. There were reports on the Ft Casey and UW meets, and Debbie reported on the coming Hamlin Park meet. Bruce reported on preparations for the Bog Slog. The next Board meeting will be held December 12 at 7:30 at Mike Schuh's. All club members are welcome to attend all Board Meetings

MONTHLY CLUB MEETING Psychology Report Election of Officers By-law Change The regular bi-monthly club meeting will be held on Thursday, November 29 at 7:00 pm at the University Library at NE 50th and Roosevelt Way. Dr. Susan Infield of the UW Psychology Department will be present and will review the conclusions from her study of orienteers. And, if it's Club Meeting in November, it must be elections. As was indicated last issue, Mike Schuh has indicated he would prefer to take a less active role next year. Several people hve expressed a willingness to serve as board members, but as of publication, we do not have a complete slate. As to Board members, Denise Klein and David Soper have agreed to continue as Event Coordinators, Debbie Newell as Mapping Coordinator, Don Atkinson as equipment and Harvey Friedman as Membership. New Board assignments include Dan Klein who will work with the Newsletter in January (and become Editor in March), Bruce McAlister will serve as one of the WSOA representatives, and Marjorie Bone will be the new Hotline coordinator. There is still lots of opportunity to help on the Board, and if you are interested in helping as an assistant, or taking charge in an area,let us know, and you will have a job.

Monthly Club Meeting - Continued Proposed By-Law Change A by-laws amendment will be proposed at the annual meeting to elect a Board instead of officers. Officers would then be elected annually by the newly elected Board. This would allow for more flexibility, and avoid the annual hunt for nominating officers. Everyone is urged to come to the meeting to review the past and decide the future of Cascade Orienteering.
[Photo: Anne York, Harvey Friedman and Carl McNabb at Philmont. The "Tooth of Time" in the background]

The temperature read 89 on the readerboard as we arrived at Denver Airport Friday afternoon. A short trip to Colorado Springs brought us to "Pinetum", the Colorado Springs home of Pat Albright and Peter Zurcher for supper and conversation. Then up early and down to New Mexico. Philmont is a Scout ranch in the northwest corner of New Mexico near Taos with terrain very reminiscent of Cle Elum Ridge, but at about 7000 ft, and with rugged scenery. The course included pine, scrub oak and aspen forest, some open fields, and lots of steep terrain with sharp gullies. At Philmont we met Anne York and Harvey Friedman who came up from Albuquerque where they had been visiting Anne's relatives. The weather was warm and sunny Saturday with cobalt blue skies, white mountains and golden aspen. Saturday was run on the Crater Lake map. When I first saw the map, I thought fantastic, no green !! Then I realized that they just hadn't printed vegetation on the map. Actual vegetation was much like Ross-Elum, with very green areas, and deep, narrow gullies. The weather Sunday was lightly overcast and cooler. The terrain was similar to Day 1, but there was a good, new, color map, Urraca Mesa. The courses were challenging both days. Ten of 23 were DNF on the Green and Orange courses on Day 1; Hanna North was 2:38 on Blue; Svere Froyen was 2:09 on Red, and most of the others who finished were over 3 hours. Harvey was 1st of ten on Orange for the combined 2 days. Although difficult, they were well set and fun courses. There were few trails; legs were varied; short and long, and required several route choices, with disaster awaiting the wrong choice. It might have been better to have made the Orange course a little easier. It would be interesting to set at least one course like this at Ross-Elum this spring. Tired, but happy, we left Philmont late Sunday for the three hour drive back to Denver. The clouds grew darker as we drove on, and the first swirling snow flakes of winter were falling as we drove into the airport.

The US Championships this year, hosted by HVO, were held at Harriman State Park, near West Point, New York on November 3, 4. Eleven local orienteers travelled east (E. Breseman, B. McAlister, D. Enger, D. Newell, R. Tharp, D. Tallent, B. MacDonald Sr, C. Moore and his wife Linda, B. Macdonald Jr, and F Veler), and were rewarded by two days of great orienteering. The weather was warm and clear, and the leaves were red and gold, and still mainly on the trees. The area, just west of West Point was largely open, rocky terrain, it offerred challenges, but was not as daunting as much of our local terrain. You can check out the map and courses at the Bog Slog Potluck. Our only local national champion this year is Fred Veler who placed first in M45A. The US Championships next year will be held in Missouri in early October, hosted by SLOC.

Peter Zurcher of the Rocky Mountain Orienteering Club and former Swiss National O'Team member will conduct an orienteering skills clinic early in 1991. Although details are not yet final, it will probably be in February at Drunken Charlie Lake and Cle Elum. Look for more details in the next issue of "Bearing 315". Space in the class may be limited, so, if you are interested, please call Debbie Newell at 488-7471.
Rick Hood has prepared aquestonairre to identify the number and type of activities, COC members would like, and the resources available within the Club. The results will be reported in Bearing 315.

Late September brings a gasp of pleasant weather to the eastern Cascades. By the time you read this freezing nights and the probability that precipitation will be snow are the actuality. EOC's last meet of 1990 was the last good day. Previous, and unusual, rain fooled some wildflowers into thinking it was Spring: patches of green, yellow, and blue were noticeable if you could get your mind off the search for orange and white.

A beginners; an intermediate (which was too easy), and a score course were available. In addition there was the opportunity to design your own course from the available score controls. Ann York, in a class by herself as usual, took advantage of this, putting her WC-PNWOF course setting skills to her own use.
Meet Director: Will Sperry Registration: Addie Graaff Start-Finish: Betty Wiberg Control Placement: Will, JoAnne Sperry Control Pick up: Kent Verbeck, Sue Williams Cookies: Addie
[Photo: Eileen Breseman finishes the Score-O at Cle Elum]
SCORE (20 controls, 2 hr limit, 78 possible points)
SELF DESIGNED (10 controls, ~4.5 km, ~ 150 m climb)

Meet Directors's comments: Ann designed a course for F-45A: 10 controls, about 4 km and about 150 m climb. The climb is excessive, but she had to choose from controls already in place. She actually picked the least climb given thir placement. Her course conformed to the guidelines of the SOFT's book Course Planning.
Meet Director: Bruce McAlister Course Design: Mike Schuh Support Crew: Margie Bone, Harvey Friedman, Rick Hood, Carl McNabb, Jim Slyfield
Good cookies, great weather, challenging courses. Thirty three orienteers, 1 future orienteer (Ryan Breseman), and 3 dogs.
FORT CASEY STATE PARK - October 7, 1990 Meet Director: Peter Golde Course Design: Jim McNamara

BOY SCOUT COURSE At the Saturday, January 5, 1991 Cascade Club meet at Woodland Park, one of the courses will be designed to satisfy the new Boy Scout requirement for a one-mile Scout level orienteering course. For details, call course designer James Siscel at 778-7202. We encourage Scout Troops who are members of Cascade or one of the other local orienteering clubs, to turn out for this meet. Other Scouts are, of course, also welcome.

Please use correct terminology where possible in Bearing 315. Those cyyptic symbols relating to couse control points on advanced courses or those words describing where the control marker is within the control circle are control descriptions, not clues. Please never refer to control description sheets as clue sheets; you do not want to give new people the wrong idea about our sport. Just because ONA provides misleading terminology does not mean Cascade OC has to. I hope the terminology is used correctly in future editions of Bearing 315. Thank you. HF

As usual, we'll start with positive roster changes as of 6 November 1990. Words of both thanks and welcome go to the following new or renewing club members: Boy Scout Troop 270, Susan Koscak, Michelle McMorran, Ross Stevens, Lloyd Sullivan. If you all don't come to meets, you'll probably not see your name in the newsletter until the next time you renew; also, membership cards are only available in person at a club function such as regular meet, meeting, pot-O-luck, but not at any ski-O meets, nor night-O meets, nor canoe-O meets (unless you are lucky), nor training events, nor workshops. If you move, don't forget to turn in a change of address card; you don't want your news-letter to be late (or lost). Now on to the fun stuff...Orienteering is a sport of navigation, true, but it is more than just going thru the woods with a map; making new friends and renewing acquaintances with familiar faces are also important factors making O' the wonderful activity that it is. Families meeting families make for travel experiences to other countries that might be otherwise too costly to afford. Contact me if you need more information....If you want more news about people in the club, you'll have to tell me something or preferably write or scribble me something that is for this column since I have such a bad memory. Only news item submitted is that Angela Tallent has qualified for the cross-country nationals, and will be off to Omaha. Good luck Angela.

CALVIN AND HOBBES / BILL WATTERSON Comic Strip Panel 1: "I PERFORMED A SCIENTIFIC EXPERIMENT TODAY." Panel 2: "YOU KNOW HOW MAPS ALWAYS SHOW NORTH AS UP AND SOUTH AS DOWN? I WANTED TO SEE IF THAT WAS TRUE OR NOT." Panel 3: "WHAT DID YOU FIND OUT?" Panel 4: "NOT MUCH. YOUR COMPASS DIDN'T SURVIVE THE TRIP SOUTH FROM THE TOP OF THE TREE." Panel 5: "LET ME KNOW WHEN YOU GET A NEW ONE. MY JUNIOR SCIENTIST BOOK SAYS NOT TO GET DISCOURAGED BY TEMPORARY SETBACKS."

Below are PNWOF meet results. Results are shown for each class with local participants. In each class, the first three finishers are shown plus any local finishers. Direct class results are shown on Page 4.
PNWOF - "The race is over" - A Norwegian's View of PNWOF

The article on Page 7 "The Race is Over " is a translation of an article from the Norwegian orienteering magazine Orientering. Much of the article is about the World Cup race at Cle Elum. The editors guessed wrong, however, about the race being over. Ragnhild B. Anderson of Norway continued in first and won the overall women's competition. However, the "hypothetical possibility occurred in the men's race with Havard Tveite coming on with a fine finish in the last three races to win the men's competition.

Interested in travelling to A meets in New England or California or Europe? Even with the United discount, fares are high. To help out, Bruce McAlister is taking names of both those interested in out-of-town meets, and those who have unused tickets or coupons. He will also watch out for special excursion fares. O-meets are excellent candidates for excursion fares. However, these fares are usually available for only a few days at a time.
Possibilities are Texas Champs, New England, and the O'Ringen Swedish 5 day and the Scottish 6-day. Let Bruce know if you're interested in attending, or if you have any coupons you can't use.
Translated and reprinted from the Norwegian Orienteering Magazine Orientering
